blocks.text: Re: Supply Chain Review for {{fact:f:E-2010-001.client}}
Dear Ms. Flores:

This letter sets out the terms on which we will review the supply chain of {{fact:f:E-2010-001.client}}. The question is a plain one: where the movement of materials, inventory, and orders costs more than it should, and what it would take to bring that cost down without putting service at risk. It follows our meetings this spring and the operating figures your office sent afterward. It states what we will do, who will do it, what we will need from you, and the commercial terms that govern the work. When you have countersigned and returned one copy, this letter is the agreement between us.
What the Review Covers

We will examine the supply chain end to end, from purchasing through inbound freight, warehousing, inventory, and order fulfillment to your customer. The work has three parts. The first is a baseline of how goods and cost actually move today, built from your purchasing records, your inventory data, and your freight and warehouse costs. The second is a diagnosis of where the money and the delay collect: the buffers held against uncertainty, the freight paid to recover lost time, the handling that adds nothing. The third is a set of recommendations, each one sized and sequenced, for taking that cost out. We will tell you what to change, in what order, and what the change is worth.

Some matters sit outside this review and are available separately if you want them. We will recommend changes to suppliers, terms, and stocking policy; we will not negotiate with your vendors or place orders on your behalf, and the decisions on people and contracts remain yours. Selection or installation of planning and warehouse systems, and any tax or customs restructuring, are not within our scope. Where our work runs up against a question we are not engaged to answer, we will say so plainly rather than answer it by assumption.
How We Will Proceed

We begin with your own records rather than a model carried in from another company. In the first weeks we build the cost and flow baseline, walk two or three of your sites, and interview the people who run purchasing, planning, and the warehouses, so that every figure we later put in front of you can be traced to one your staff already recognizes. From that base we frame the options and test each against your service commitments before any of them reaches you as a recommendation. Work begins on {{fact:f:E-2010-001.start}}, and we expect the review to run about four months from that point.
The Team

Michael Walker, Engagement Manager, runs the review day to day and is your point of contact for schedule, data, and site access; he is the person to call when something needs to move. Deborah Gordon, Associate, leads the analysis and the drafting of the findings. Daniel Leach, Research Associate, assembles and reconciles the operating data and is the one your finance and logistics staff will work with directly on the numbers. I review the analysis and the recommendations before they reach you, and I am accountable to you for the quality of both.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for the review is {{fact:f:E-2010-001.fee}}, fixed for the scope described above. We invoice monthly for the work performed in the preceding month, and each invoice is payable within thirty days of its date. Reasonable travel and out-of-pocket expenses are billed at cost and shown separately. Work beyond the scope of this letter is not undertaken on assumption; if you ask us for something this letter does not cover, we will price it and agree it with you before we begin.
Term and Termination

This agreement takes effect on the date you sign below and continues until we deliver the final report and you have paid the final invoice. Either party may end it on thirty days' written notice to the other. On termination you will pay us for the work performed and the expenses incurred through the effective date, and we will hand over our work product in the state it then stands, together with a short written account of where the review had reached.
Confidentiality

We will hold the information you give us in confidence and use it only for this review. Access is limited to the team named above and to those in our office whose work requires it. We will not disclose your information outside the firm without your written consent, except where a court or a regulator with jurisdiction compels it, and in that event we will tell you first if we are permitted to. The obligation survives the end of the engagement.
Limitation of Liability

Our liability to you arising out of this engagement, whether framed in contract or in tort, will not exceed the total fees you pay us under it, except in the case of our fraud or willful misconduct. Neither party will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss, including lost profits, whether or not the possibility was made known. Our analysis rests on the accuracy and completeness of the information you provide; we will test what we reasonably can, but we do not audit it.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which our principal office is located, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Before either party begins proceedings, we will try to settle any dispute by discussion between senior representatives of the two sides. This letter is the entire agreement between us on its subject and may be amended only in writing signed by both.

If these terms match your understanding, please sign both copies where indicated, keep one for your file, and return the other to me. If any part of them does not, telephone me and we will settle it before you sign. We are glad to be taking on this work, and the team looks forward to getting started with your people at Poole, Mcknight and Rush.
Very truly yours,
To: Daniel Leach, Michael Walker
From: Deborah Gordon
Re: {{fact:f:E-2010-001.client}} supply chain review, kickoff and the first two weeks

The letter is signed and we start the supply chain review for {{fact:f:E-2010-001.client}} on {{fact:f:E-2010-001.start}}. The job is narrow, and worth stating in one line: find where the supply chain costs the client more than it should, and show what it takes to bring that cost down without hurting service. Four months, one report, built entirely on the client's own purchasing, inventory, and freight data. Our contact is Sandra Flores, the Chief Operating Officer, and she is the single door to the client. Anything that goes to the client goes through Sandra Flores, and nobody calls a plant or a warehouse manager without her knowing first.

A word on scope, because it gets tested in the corridor. We recommend on suppliers, terms, and stocking policy; we do not negotiate with vendors, we do not place orders, and we do not touch systems selection. If any of that gets asked for on site, the answer is that we will scope it, not that we have started it. Keep the work to the review we were hired to do.
Lines of Work

items: Data and baseline. Daniel Leach pulls the purchasing, inventory, and freight data and builds the picture of how cost and goods actually move today. Where a number will not reconcile, that gap is a finding, and I want it flagged in the first pass, not buried later.

Flow and process. We map the physical flow across the sites: what is bought, where it is held, how it moves, and where it waits. Build this from the records and the walk-throughs, not from the process someone recites from memory.
Cost of buffers. Daniel Leach ties the freight, the safety stock, and the handling back to the flow, so we can say what the current design costs and where it costs it.
Interviews and access. Michael Walker owns the schedule and the site access. If the interview load grows past what we planned, tell Michael Walker before it becomes a problem, not after.
Findings and report. I own the analysis and the writing. Nothing reaches the client as a recommendation until the baseline supports it.
Client cadence. A short written status goes to Sandra Flores every two weeks, routed through Michael Walker, and it goes out even in a thin week so the client is never guessing about where we are.

blocks.text: First Two Weeks

items: Daniel Leach drafts the data request, itemized, with the fields and their definitions attached, and I send it to Sandra Flores by the end of the week.
Michael Walker confirms the first two site visits and holds the interview slots with purchasing, planning, and the warehouses at Poole, Mcknight and Rush.
We agree the site and interview list before we ask the client for a single calendar slot, so we are not renegotiating access once the visits have started.
Nobody quotes a cost or a saving to anyone at the client until the baseline reconciles.

blocks.text: Two things I want settled by Friday: the data request and the site list, both agreed and both out to Sandra Flores. The client has asked for a named contact on each site before we arrive, and Michael Walker is gathering those now. With the request and the list locked this week, the on-site work has a clean start and we are not chasing access once the visits have begun.
Supply Chain Review for {{fact:f:E-2010-001.client}}

items: A progress briefing for Sandra Flores, Chief Operating Officer
Engagement commenced {{fact:f:E-2010-001.start}}
Prepared by the engagement team; presented by Deborah Gordon
Early in the work: the baseline is under construction and there are no findings yet

blocks.text: What You Asked Us To Review

items: Where the supply chain costs more than it should, end to end
Purchasing, inbound freight, warehousing, inventory, and order fulfillment
A baseline of how cost and goods move today, built from your own records
The buffers, the expedited freight, and the handling that carry avoidable cost
Recommendations that are sized and sequenced, not a wish list

blocks.text: How We Are Working

items: We start from your purchasing, inventory, and freight data, not a template
Daniel Leach is assembling and reconciling the operating data
Michael Walker is arranging the site visits and the interviews
Two or three sites at Poole, Mcknight and Rush walked, with purchasing and warehouse staff
Every figure we show you will tie back to one your team already recognizes

blocks.text: What We Are Seeing So Far

items: Inventory sits longer in two of the regional warehouses than the service level needs
A sizable share of inbound freight is moving on expedited terms to recover lost time
Safety stock looks set by habit in several categories rather than by demand
These are observations from the data so far, not findings, and none is sized yet

blocks.text: Where the Work Stands

items: Purchasing and inventory data received and being reconciled
Freight and warehouse cost baseline under construction
First site visits scheduled and the interviews beginning
Roughly a quarter of the way through a review planned for about four months
No target design is on the table, and none should be read into this briefing

blocks.text: What Comes Next

items: Close the baseline and confirm it against your management accounts
Walk the remaining sites and finish the first round of interviews
Frame the cost drivers for discussion at the next working session
Questions to Deborah Gordon; data questions direct to Daniel Leach

blocks.text: Sandra Flores, with Michael Walker copied:

Thank you for the time yesterday. The briefing gave us a clean read on where you want the review focused, and it matches what the early data is telling us for {{fact:f:E-2010-001.client}}. We have the purchasing history and the first inventory extract, and Daniel Leach has started reconciling them against the freight file. Nothing in the numbers so far changes the plan we walked you through.

One request. To finish the baseline we need the inventory movement by warehouse and SKU for the last full year, and the freight invoices broken out by lane rather than by carrier. Daniel Leach can work from either cut on the freight, but the lane view is what lets us see where the expedited cost is really landing. If your logistics group at Poole, Mcknight and Rush can send both by the end of next week, we hold to schedule.

On the site visits, Michael Walker will confirm the two dates with your assistant this week and hold a half-day either side for the interviews that tend to slip. Nothing else is needed from you today; I will send the interview list on Friday with the times, so calendars can be set.
Deborah Gordon
Following up, Sandra Flores, with Michael Walker still copied:

Thank you, the inventory movement file came through this morning and it is exactly the cut we needed. Daniel Leach has it loaded, and the reconciliation against the freight data is under way. I expect the baseline to hold together by the middle of next week, and we will bring the whole cost picture to the working session rather than trickle it out in pieces. One small thing Daniel Leach caught: the inventory file uses the old warehouse codes for the two southern sites. We can map them, but a current code list would remove any doubt if one is handy.

Two open items on our side. The freight invoices are still arriving by carrier rather than by lane, so Daniel Leach is rebuilding the lane view by hand for now; if that cut turns out to be easy for your team at Poole, Mcknight and Rush to pull, it would save us a step, but please do not hold anything up for it. And we are one interview short on the purchasing side, which Michael Walker is chasing with your assistant.
Nothing is needed from you today on {{fact:f:E-2010-001.client}}. I will confirm the working-session agenda with you early next week.
Deborah Gordon
Working Session: Supply Chain Baseline and Early Cost Drivers
Minutes recorded by Michael Walker. Subject: {{fact:f:E-2010-001.client}} supply chain review, working session held with the client.
Attendees

items: Michael Walker, Engagement Manager
Deborah Gordon, Associate
Daniel Leach, Research Associate
Sandra Flores, Chief Operating Officer, Poole, Mcknight and Rush

blocks.text: Baseline and Data

Daniel Leach took the group through the state of the baseline, which has come together well over the past two weeks. The purchasing and inventory data now reconcile against the management accounts at the category level, and the freight file ties at the carrier level. What is still soft is the lane-level view of freight, where a good share of the invoices arrive coded by carrier rather than by route, so Daniel Leach has been rebuilding the lane picture by hand. He was careful to say that the numbers behind the expedited-freight cost are close but not yet final, and that he would rather hold that figure back a few days than put something in front of everyone that he already expects to move. Sandra Flores offered to have her logistics team pull the lane coding at source, which would save the manual step, and we agreed she would check what is feasible.
Early Cost Drivers

Deborah Gordon then walked through the patterns the baseline is starting to show, and there are three worth recording. Inventory is sitting longer than the service level requires in two of the regional warehouses, which points to safety stock set by habit rather than by demand. A meaningful part of the inbound freight is moving on expedited terms, which usually means the plan and the replenishment are not talking to each other early enough. And ordering in several categories is happening more often, and in smaller quantities, than the volumes justify. Deborah Gordon was clear that these are the current read of the data and not yet findings; each one needs the remaining interviews and a firm number behind it before we put it to the client as something to act on. Sandra Flores recognized the warehouse pattern from her own experience and gave us two examples we had not captured, which Daniel Leach noted for the analysis.
Open Items and Next Steps

We closed by agreeing what has to happen before the next session. The lane-level freight view has to be settled, either from source or by finishing the manual rebuild. The two purchasing interviews still outstanding need to be done, since they bear directly on the ordering pattern. And Sandra Flores asked that we not characterize the results to the warehouse and purchasing managers before those interviews, so the sessions stay diagnostic and do not turn into negotiations. We agreed, and I will hold the team to it.
Action Items

rows: Check whether the lane-level freight coding can be pulled at source and confirm what the logistics team can do
Finish the lane-level freight rebuild and close the baseline
Complete the two outstanding purchasing interviews and log the ordering-pattern detail
Hold the early cost drivers inside the team until the interviews are complete
